title: Rendering Pipeline and Differentiable Training

learningObjectives.description: Trace the 3DGS rendering pipeline from 3D Gaussians to final pixel colors, identifying the projection (Σ′ = JWΣW⊤J⊤), tile assignment, depth sort, and alpha compositing steps
Explain why per-tile depth sorting is an approximation, identify the conditions under which it produces visible artifacts, and state why it is used despite this limitation
Distinguish cloning from splitting in adaptive densification and identify the specific positional-gradient signal and Gaussian scale condition that triggers each operation
Explain why the photometric loss combines L1 and D-SSIM terms, identify what artifact each term is sensitive to, and state the role of opacity resets in preventing opacity fog

content:
## From 3D Gaussians to a 2D Image The 3DGS rendering pipeline is a carefully engineered rasterizer that trades the exactness of ray marching for real-time performance. Understanding it precisely matters because the rendering pipeline is also the backward pass through which gradients flow during training. ## Step 1: Projection — 3D Gaussians to 2D Splats A 3D Gaussian with mean \(\mu\) and covariance \(\Sigma\) projects to a 2D Gaussian on the image plane. Given viewing transform \(W\) and Jacobian of the projection \(J\): \[\Sigma' = J W \Sigma W^\top J^\top\] The 3D ellipsoid becomes a 2D ellipse ("splat") in screen space. The projected mean \(\mu'\) is simply the perspective projection of \(\mu\). ## Step 2: Tile-Based Rasterization The screen is divided into a grid of **16×16 pixel tiles**. For each Gaussian, 3DGS determines which tiles its 2D extent intersects and creates a list entry per tile. **Key steps:** 1. **Sorting**: within each tile, all contributing Gaussians are sorted by depth (front to back) using a fast radix sort. 2. **Alpha accumulation**: each tile is processed in parallel on GPU with one thread per pixel, accumulating color front-to-back. Sorting is per-tile, not globally, which is an approximation — but in practice it produces minimal artifacts while enabling orders-of-magnitude speedup over exact sorting. ## Step 3: Alpha Compositing The rendered color at pixel \(\mathbf{p}\) is the alpha-composited sum over all \(N\) depth-sorted Gaussians visible in that pixel: \[c(\mathbf{p}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i\, \alpha_i'(\mathbf{p}) \prod_{j=1}^{i-1} \bigl(1 - \alpha_j'(\mathbf{p})\bigr)\] where: - \(c_i\) is the view-dependent color of Gaussian \(i\) (evaluated via SH) - \(\alpha_i'(\mathbf{p}) = \sigma_{\alpha,i} \cdot f_i'(\mathbf{p})\) is the effective alpha at pixel \(\mathbf{p}\), combining opacity and the 2D Gaussian density - \(\prod_{j
① Render
Rasterize from a training viewpoint
② Loss
\(\mathcal{L} = (1{-}\lambda)\mathcal{L}_1 + \lambda\mathcal{L}_{D\text{-}SSIM}\)
③ Backprop
Differentiate through the rasterizer
④ Update
Adam step on \(\mu, \Sigma, \sigma_\alpha, \text{SH}\)
↺ repeat for each training iteration
- **Render** — rasterize the current Gaussians from a randomly sampled training viewpoint using the tile rasterizer. - **Compute loss** — the photometric objective combines a per-pixel absolute error term with a structural similarity term: $$\mathcal{L} = (1-\lambda)\mathcal{L}_1 + \lambda\mathcal{L}_{D\text{-}SSIM}$$ \(\mathcal{L}_1\) penalizes mean absolute colour error per pixel. \(\mathcal{L}_{D\text{-}SSIM}\) penalizes structural and contrast differences that \(\mathcal{L}_1\) alone is insensitive to — blurring and edge misalignment both raise it even when the average colour is correct. Typical \(\lambda = 0.2\). - **Backpropagate** — differentiate through the differentiable rasterizer to obtain \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\mu\), \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\Sigma\), \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\sigma_\alpha\), and \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\mathbf{f}_{SH}\) for every visible Gaussian. - **Update** — take an Adam step on all Gaussian parameters: \(\mu, \Sigma, \sigma_\alpha, \text{SH}\). **3. Every \(N\) iterations:** run **adaptive densification** (described below). ## Adaptive Densification Gradient descent alone changes Gaussian attributes but not their count. Densification periodically adjusts the population: **What is a high positional gradient?** During training, the photometric loss \(\mathcal{L}\) is compared against each training image. The gradient \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\mu\) measures how much the loss would decrease if a Gaussian moved. A large value signals that the Gaussian is in the wrong place — the scene has structure nearby that it is failing to cover.
Positional Gradient
The amber region shows unrecovered photometric signal. The arrow is ∂L/∂μ — pointing from the Gaussian toward the region it should cover. When this magnitude stays high across iterations, gradient descent alone cannot fix it: the Gaussian needs to be cloned or split.
### Cloning When a small Gaussian has high positional gradient (the loss strongly wants it to move), it is **under-reconstructing** the region.
Solution
Duplicate the Gaussian — two copies converge to slightly different positions, together covering the under-reconstructed region.
### Splitting When a large Gaussian has high positional gradient, it is **over-reconstructing** — one blob covers a region that needs finer detail.
Solution
Replace with two smaller Gaussians placed along the gradient direction, each covering part of the over-reconstructed region at higher fidelity.
### Pruning Gaussians with opacity \(\sigma_\alpha\) below a threshold are nearly invisible and waste parameters. They are deleted. Periodically, all opacities are reset to near-zero and re-learned — this prevents "opacity fog" where Gaussians persist with low but non-zero opacity. ### Why Densification Matters for Compression The final Gaussian count after training is not fixed — it emerges from the densification history. Methods that improve densification (Module 3) directly reduce the total parameter count before any encoding, which is often more impactful than encoding improvements applied to a fixed representation.

references.label: Kerbl et al. 2023
references.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering

terms.name: 2D screen-space covariance
terms.desc: The covariance of the Gaussian after it has been projected onto the image plane. Used directly by the tile rasterizer to compute each Gaussian's screen footprint.

terms.name: Projection Jacobian
terms.desc: Local linear approximation of the (non-linear) perspective projection at the Gaussian's mean position. Converts 3D displacements into 2D screen-space displacements.

terms.name: World-to-camera transform
terms.desc: Rotation + translation that moves the Gaussian from world coordinates into camera space. Applied before projection so Σ is expressed relative to the camera.

terms.name: 3D world-space covariance
terms.desc: The full 3D covariance matrix of the Gaussian (= R S S⊤ R⊤). Encodes shape and orientation before any camera transform is applied.

terms.name: Transposes (error propagation)
terms.desc: The A M A⊤ pattern propagates covariance through a linear map A. Here A = JW, so the full transform is (JW) Σ (JW)⊤ — the standard formula for how uncertainty transforms under a linear mapping.

terms.name: Final pixel colour
terms.desc: The rendered RGB value at pixel p, produced by blending contributions from all Gaussians whose screen footprint covers p, sorted front-to-back by depth.

terms.name: Gaussian colour
terms.desc: The view-dependent RGB colour of Gaussian i, evaluated from its spherical harmonic coefficients at the current viewing direction.

terms.name: Effective alpha at pixel p
terms.desc: Opacity of Gaussian i at pixel p — the product of its learned opacity σ_α and its 2D Gaussian falloff evaluated at p using the projected covariance Σ′.

terms.name: Transmittance
terms.desc: Fraction of light that passes through all Gaussians in front of i without being absorbed. Starts at 1 for the frontmost Gaussian and decreases as each successive layer blocks more light.

terms.name: Front-to-back sum
terms.desc: Gaussians are sorted by depth (nearest first) before compositing. The renderer can early-exit once accumulated transmittance falls below a threshold, skipping occluded Gaussians entirely.

terms.name: Total training loss
terms.desc: The scalar value minimised by gradient descent at each iteration. Computed by comparing the rendered image against the ground-truth photograph from the same viewpoint. Lower is better; zero would mean perfect reconstruction.

terms.name: L1 colour term
terms.desc: Mean absolute difference between rendered and ground-truth pixel colours. Robust to outlier pixels and computationally cheap. Alone it tends to produce slightly blurry results because averaging across many possible sharp images minimises L1 loss.

terms.name: D-SSIM structural term
terms.desc: Dissimilarity measured by SSIM — a perceptual metric sensitive to local luminance, contrast, and structure. Penalises blurring and edge misalignment that L1 averages away. The D prefix means 1 − SSIM so that minimising the loss maximises similarity.

terms.name: Loss balance weight
terms.desc: Trades off colour fidelity (L1) vs. structural fidelity (D-SSIM). The original 3DGS paper uses λ = 0.2, meaning 80% L1 and 20% D-SSIM. Higher λ sharpens edges at the cost of occasional colour artefacts; lower λ is smoother but may blur fine detail.
